instruction: Show me modern CMake patterns. How do I write CMakeLists.txt the right way?
output:
<think>
Modern CMake (3.x) is target-based rather than directory-based. The key shift: instead of setting global variables (include_directories, link_libraries), you attach properties to targets. This makes dependencies composable вҖ” when you link to a library target, its include paths, compile definitions, and dependencies propagate automatically.

Key modern patterns: target_link_libraries for everything, generator expressions, INTERFACE vs PRIVATE vs PUBLIC, FetchContent for dependencies, presets.
</think>

Here's modern CMake done right:

```cmake
# CMakeLists.txt (root)
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.21)
project(MyProject VERSION 1.0.0 LANGUAGES CXX)

# ============================================================
# 1. Project-wide settings
# ============================================================
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)
set(CMAKE_CXX_EXTENSIONS OFF)  # No GNU extensions
set(CMAKE_EXPORT_COMPILE_COMMANDS ON)  # For LSP/clangd

# ============================================================
# 2. Library target (modern target-based approach)
# ============================================================
add_library(mylib
    src/core.cpp
    src/parser.cpp
    src/utils.cpp
)

# PUBLIC: consumers also get these
# PRIVATE: only this target uses these
# INTERFACE: only consumers get these (header-only libs)
target_include_directories(mylib
    PUBLIC  $<BUILD_INTERFACE:${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include>
            $<INSTALL_INTERFACE:include>
    PRIVATE ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/src
)

target_compile_features(mylib PUBLIC cxx_std_20)
target_compile_definitions(mylib PRIVATE MYLIB_BUILDING)

# ============================================================
# 3. Executable target
# ============================================================
add_executable(myapp src/main.cpp)
target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE mylib)  # Gets include dirs automatically!

# ============================================================
# 4. FetchContent for dependencies (no submodules needed)
# ============================================================
include(FetchContent)

FetchContent_Declare(
    fmt
    GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/fmtlib/fmt.git
    GIT_TAG 10.2.1
)

FetchContent_Declare(
    nlohmann_json
    GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/nlohmann/json.git
    GIT_TAG v3.11.3
)

FetchContent_MakeAvailable(fmt nlohmann_json)

target_link_libraries(mylib PUBLIC fmt::fmt nlohmann_json::nlohmann_json)

# ============================================================
# 5. Testing
# ============================================================
option(BUILD_TESTS "Build tests" ON)

if(BUILD_TESTS)
    enable_testing()
    FetchContent_Declare(
        googletest
        GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/google/googletest.git
        GIT_TAG v1.14.0
    )
    FetchContent_MakeAvailable(googletest)

    add_executable(mylib_tests tests/test_core.cpp tests/test_parser.cpp)
    target_link_libraries(mylib_tests PRIVATE mylib GTest::gtest_main)
    
    include(GoogleTest)
    gtest_discover_tests(mylib_tests)
endif()

# ============================================================
# 6. Install rules
# ============================================================
include(GNUInstallDirs)
install(TARGETS mylib myapp
    LIBRARY DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}
    ARCHIVE DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR}
    RUNTIME DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_BINDIR}
)
install(DIRECTORY include/ DESTINATION ${CMAKE_INSTALL_INCLUDEDIR})
```

```bash
# Build commands:
cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
ctest --test-dir build

# With presets (CMakePresets.json):
cmake --preset release
cmake --build --preset release
```

Modern CMake rules: always use target_* commands (never directory-level), use PUBLIC/PRIVATE/INTERFACE correctly, use FetchContent for dependencies, generator expressions for build/install differences, and CMake presets for reproducible configurations.

instruction: Compare vcpkg and Conan for C++ dependency management. When should I use each?
output:
<think>
vcpkg (Microsoft) and Conan (JFrog) are the two main C++ package managers. They have different philosophies:

vcpkg: "ports" model, builds from source, integrates deeply with CMake/MSBuild. Default: all packages share one version set per "triplet" (like a global environment). Manifest mode (vcpkg.json) enables per-project dependencies.

Conan: more like pip/npm, binary package manager, supports multiple versions/configurations simultaneously, strong ABI compatibility management, works with any build system.

I should compare them fairly and give clear guidance.
</think>

Here's a practical comparison of vcpkg and Conan:

```json
// vcpkg: vcpkg.json (manifest mode)
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/microsoft/vcpkg-tool/main/docs/vcpkg.schema.json",
  "name": "my-project",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "dependencies": [
    "fmt",
    "nlohmann-json",
    { "name": "boost-asio", "version>=": "1.83.0" },
    { "name": "openssl", "platform": "!windows" },
    { "name": "sqlite3", "features": ["json1"] }
  ]
}
```

```ini
# Conan: conanfile.txt (simple)
[requires]
fmt/10.2.1
nlohmann_json/3.11.3
boost/1.83.0
openssl/3.2.0
sqlite3/3.44.2

[generators]
CMakeDeps
CMakeToolchain

[options]
boost/*:without_python=True
sqlite3/*:enable_json1=True
```

```python
# Conan: conanfile.py (advanced)
from conan import ConanFile
from conan.tools.cmake import cmake_layout

class MyProject(ConanFile):
    settings = "os", "compiler", "build_type", "arch"
    requires = (
        "fmt/10.2.1",
        "nlohmann_json/3.11.3",
        "boost/1.83.0",
    )
    generators = "CMakeDeps", "CMakeToolchain"
    
    def configure(self):
        self.options["boost"].without_python = True
    
    def layout(self):
        cmake_layout(self)
```

```cmake
# CMake integration вҖ” both work similarly:

# vcpkg (auto-integration via toolchain):
# cmake -B build -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=[vcpkg-root]/scripts/buildsystems/vcpkg.cmake
find_package(fmt REQUIRED)
find_package(nlohmann_json REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE fmt::fmt nlohmann_json::nlohmann_json)

# Conan (run conan install first, then):
# cmake -B build -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=build/conan_toolchain.cmake
find_package(fmt REQUIRED)
find_package(nlohmann_json REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(myapp PRIVATE fmt::fmt nlohmann_json::nlohmann_json)
```

```
Comparison:
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ Feature             в”Ӯ vcpkg        в”Ӯ Conan            в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ Package count       в”Ӯ ~2300        в”Ӯ ~1700 (center)   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Binary caching      в”Ӯ Yes          в”Ӯ Yes (stronger)   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ ABI management      в”Ӯ Triplets     в”Ӯ Full settings    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Multiple versions   в”Ӯ Limited      в”Ӯ Full support     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Build systems       в”Ӯ CMake/MSBuildв”Ӯ Any              в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Custom packages     в”Ӯ Port files   в”Ӯ conanfile.py     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Corporate proxy     в”Ӯ Git mirror   в”Ӯ Artifactory      в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Learning curve      в”Ӯ Lower        в”Ӯ Higher           в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Windows experience  в”Ӯ Excellent    в”Ӯ Good             в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ Cross-compilation   в”Ӯ Triplets     в”Ӯ Profiles         в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ CI caching          в”Ӯ Good         в”Ӯ Excellent        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Choose vcpkg: Windows/MSBuild, simpler setup, Microsoft ecosystem
Choose Conan: multi-platform, need multiple versions/configs, CI binary caching
```

vcpkg is simpler for Windows-centric projects with CMake/MSBuild. Conan is more powerful for cross-platform projects, CI binary caching, and when you need fine-grained ABI control. Both integrate cleanly with CMake via find_package().

instruction: When should I use header-only vs compiled libraries in C++? What are the tradeoffs?
output:
<think>
Header-only libraries are entirely in .hpp files with no separate compilation step. Compiled libraries have .cpp files that compile into .a/.so/.lib/.dll files. Both approaches have clear tradeoffs.

Header-only pros: simple distribution (just copy headers), no build system integration needed, enables full inlining, no ABI compatibility issues.

Header-only cons: slower compilation (code recompiled in every TU that includes it), larger binary size (multiple copies unless linker deduplicates), longer rebuild times for large projects, exposes implementation details.

Compiled library pros: faster compilation (compiled once), smaller binaries, implementation hiding, stable ABI possible.

Compiled library cons: needs build system integration, ABI compatibility issues between compiler versions, harder to distribute.
</think>

Here's when to use each approach:

```cpp
// ============================================================
// Header-only library pattern
// ============================================================

// mylib/include/mylib/math.hpp
#pragma once

namespace mylib {

// Template functions MUST be header-only (or explicitly instantiated)
template<typename T>
T clamp(T value, T min_val, T max_val) {
    if (value < min_val) return min_val;
    if (value > max_val) return max_val;
    return value;
}

// Small, frequently called functions benefit from inlining
inline double lerp(double a, double b, double t) {
    return a + t * (b - a);
}

// Larger functions in headers: use inline to avoid ODR violations
inline std::string format_duration(int seconds) {
    int h = seconds / 3600;
    int m = (seconds % 3600) / 60;
    int s = seconds % 60;
    char buf[32];
    snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "%02d:%02d:%02d", h, m, s);
    return buf;
}

} // namespace mylib

// ============================================================
// Compiled library pattern
// ============================================================

// mylib/include/mylib/database.hpp (header вҖ” interface only)
#pragma once
#include <string>
#include <memory>

namespace mylib {

class Database {
public:
    Database(const std::string& connection_string);
    ~Database();
    
    bool execute(const std::string& sql);
    
private:
    struct Impl;              // Pimpl вҖ” hides implementation
    std::unique_ptr<Impl> impl_;
};

} // namespace mylib

// mylib/src/database.cpp (compiled вҖ” implementation)
// #include "mylib/database.hpp"
// #include <sqlite3.h>  // Heavy dependency hidden from consumers
//
// struct mylib::Database::Impl {
//     sqlite3* db = nullptr;
// };
// ... implementation ...

// ============================================================
// Decision guide
// ============================================================

/*
Use HEADER-ONLY when:
  - Template-heavy code (must be header-only)
  - Small utility functions (<20 lines)
  - Want zero build system friction
  - Performance-critical code needing inlining
  - Library is small overall (<5000 lines)
  - Examples: nlohmann/json, Catch2, spdlog (optional)

Use COMPILED LIBRARY when:
  - Large implementation (>5000 lines)
  - Heavy internal dependencies (OpenSSL, database drivers)
  - Want to hide implementation (Pimpl pattern)
  - Need stable ABI (shared libraries / .dll)
  - Compilation time matters (large project, many consumers)
  - Examples: Boost.Asio (compiled mode), OpenCV, Qt

Hybrid approach (best of both):
  - Header-only by default, compiled optionally
  - Use #ifdef MYLIB_HEADER_ONLY to switch
  - spdlog does this: header-only for simple use,
    compiled for large projects
*/
```

Use header-only for small utilities, templates, and easy distribution. Use compiled libraries for large implementations, heavy dependencies, and when build time matters. The hybrid approach (header-only default with optional compiled mode) gives users the choice.

instruction: How do I set up cross-compilation in CMake for targeting different platforms?
output:
<think>
Cross-compilation in CMake requires a toolchain file that specifies the target system, compiler, and sysroot. CMake uses this to configure paths and compiler flags for the target platform.

Common scenarios: LinuxвҶ’ARM (embedded), LinuxвҶ’Windows (MinGW), macOSвҶ’iOS, anyвҶ’WebAssembly.

The toolchain file sets CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME, CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR, CMAKE_C_COMPILER, CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER, and CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH. CMake then knows not to run target executables during the build (can't run ARM binaries on x86).
</think>

Here's how to set up cross-compilation with CMake:

```cmake
# ============================================================
# Toolchain file: toolchains/arm-linux.cmake
# ============================================================

# Target system
set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME Linux)
set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR aarch64)

# Cross-compiler
set(CMAKE_C_COMPILER aarch64-linux-gnu-gcc)
set(CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER aarch64-linux-gnu-g++)
set(CMAKE_AR aarch64-linux-gnu-ar)
set(CMAKE_RANLIB aarch64-linux-gnu-ranlib)

# Sysroot (target system libraries)
# set(CMAKE_SYSROOT /path/to/aarch64-sysroot)

# Search paths: only search target sysroot, not host
set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_PROGRAM NEVER)   # Use host programs (cmake, etc.)
set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_LIBRARY ONLY)    # Use target libraries
set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_INCLUDE ONLY)    # Use target headers
set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_PACKAGE ONLY)    # Use target packages
```

```cmake
# ============================================================
# Toolchain file: toolchains/wasm.cmake (WebAssembly)
# ============================================================

set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME Emscripten)
set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR wasm32)

# Emscripten provides its own toolchain
# set(CMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE /path/to/emsdk/upstream/emscripten/cmake/Modules/Platform/Emscripten.cmake)
```

```cmake
# ============================================================
# Toolchain file: toolchains/mingw-w64.cmake (Linux вҶ’ Windows)
# ============================================================

set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME Windows)
set(CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR x86_64)

set(CMAKE_C_COMPILER x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc)
set(CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER x86_64-w64-mingw32-g++)
set(CMAKE_RC_COMPILER x86_64-w64-mingw32-windres)

set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH /usr/x86_64-w64-mingw32)
set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_PROGRAM NEVER)
set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_LIBRARY ONLY)
set(CMAKE_FIND_ROOT_PATH_MODE_INCLUDE ONLY)
```

```bash
# Usage:
cmake -B build-arm -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=toolchains/arm-linux.cmake
cmake --build build-arm

cmake -B build-win -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=toolchains/mingw-w64.cmake
cmake --build build-win

# With CMake presets (modern approach):
# CMakePresets.json:
# {
#   "configurePresets": [{
#     "name": "arm-release",
#     "toolchainFile": "toolchains/arm-linux.cmake",
#     "cacheVariables": { "CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE": "Release" }
#   }]
# }
# cmake --preset arm-release
```

```cmake
# ============================================================
# Handling cross-compilation in CMakeLists.txt
# ============================================================

# Check if cross-compiling
if(CMAKE_CROSSCOMPILING)
    message(STATUS "Cross-compiling for ${CMAKE_SYSTEM_NAME} (${CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR})")
    
    # Can't run target executables during build
    # Use IMPORTED targets for host tools needed during build:
    # find_program(PROTOC protoc)  # Finds HOST protoc
endif()

# Platform-specific source files
target_sources(mylib PRIVATE
    src/core.cpp
    $<$<PLATFORM_ID:Linux>:src/platform_linux.cpp>
    $<$<PLATFORM_ID:Windows>:src/platform_windows.cpp>
    $<$<PLATFORM_ID:Darwin>:src/platform_macos.cpp>
)
```

Cross-compilation requires a toolchain file specifying target compiler, system, and search paths. Use CMAKE_CROSSCOMPILING to guard host-only logic, generator expressions for platform-specific sources, and CMake presets for reproducible cross-compile configurations.
